name: Grand Canyon National Park
heroCaption: Grand Canyon from the South Rim — Photo: National Park Service

alerts.title: Heat Kills Below the Rim
alerts.message: Do NOT attempt to hike from the rim to the river and back in a single day. Every summer, hikers die attempting this. Inner canyon temps exceed 120°F. Carry at least 1 gallon of water per person per day below the rim.

alerts.title: North Rim Seasonal Closure
alerts.message: The North Rim is closed to vehicles mid-October through mid-May. The road closes at the first significant snowfall. South Rim is open year-round.

alerts.title: Limited Cell Service
alerts.message: Cell service works at South Rim developed areas (Verizon best). Zero coverage below the rim and at North Rim. Download offline maps and tell someone your plan.

trails.name: Bright Angel Trail
trails.description: The Grand Canyon's most popular corridor trail. Starts right next to Bright Angel Lodge on the South Rim. Seasonal water at 1.5-Mile and 3-Mile Resthouses (May–Sep). Indian Garden (now Havasupai Gardens) at 4.6mi has year-round water. Do NOT go to the river and back in one day.
tips: Rule of thirds: For every hour you hike down, plan 2 hours to hike back up. The return is brutal.
Turnaround points: 1.5-Mile Resthouse (3mi RT, 2–3hr) or 3-Mile Resthouse (6mi RT, 4–6hr) are smart day hike goals.
Water: Seasonal spigots at 1.5-Mi and 3-Mi Resthouses (May–Sep only). Year-round at Havasupai Gardens.
Start before dawn in summer. Inner canyon hits 110°F+ by noon. Hike between 7pm–10am or not at all.

trails.name: South Kaibab Trail
trails.description: The most scenic corridor trail. Follows a ridgeline with panoramic 360° views the entire way. Steeper and more exposed than Bright Angel. NO water anywhere on this trail. Best used as a descent route with Bright Angel as the ascent.
tips: NO WATER on this trail. Not a drop. Carry everything you need.
Best day hike strategy: Down South Kaibab to Cedar Ridge (3mi RT), then back up. Views are unreal.
Classic overnight: Down South Kaibab, stay at Phantom Ranch or Bright Angel Campground, up Bright Angel next day.
Trailhead access: No private vehicles. Take the Kaibab/Rim Route (Orange) shuttle from the Visitor Center.

trails.name: North Kaibab Trail
trails.description: The only maintained trail from the North Rim to the river. Passes through Roaring Springs, Cottonwood Campground, and Ribbon Falls before reaching Phantom Ranch. This is the North Rim half of the Rim-to-Rim route. Much less crowded than South Rim trails.
tips: Day hike to Coconino Overlook (1.4mi RT) or Supai Tunnel (4mi RT) for manageable North Rim options.
Roaring Springs (9.4mi RT) is the water source for both rims. Spectacular but grueling return.
North Rim is 1,000ft higher than South Rim. Trail starts at 8,241ft. Expect cooler temps and more shade.
Mule trains have right-of-way. Step to the inside of the trail and let them pass.

trails.name: Rim Trail
trails.description: Paved path along the South Rim from Hermit's Rest to South Kaibab Trailhead. Walk any section you want. Best stretches: Mather Point to Yavapai Geology Museum (0.7mi), or Maricopa Point to Hopi Point for sunset (1.3mi). Mostly flat, wheelchair-accessible between Verkamp's and Bright Angel Lodge.
tips: Best section for sunset: Walk from Maricopa Point to Hopi Point along the rim. Arrive 45 min early for a spot.
You don't have to walk the whole thing. Hop on/off the free shuttle at any viewpoint.
Mostly paved between the Village and Hermit's Rest. A few unpaved sections east of Pipe Creek Vista.
Early morning: Walk east from Mather Point toward Yaki Point for sunrise with almost no one around.

trails.name: Rim to Rim
trails.description: The classic Grand Canyon through-hike. North Kaibab down to Phantom Ranch, then up Bright Angel (or South Kaibab) to the South Rim. Most do it in 2–3 days with a night at Phantom Ranch or Bright Angel Campground. North-to-South is preferred (more downhill overall, North Rim is 1,000ft higher).
tips: Direction matters: North-to-South is easier. The North Rim is 8,241ft vs South Rim at 6,860ft, so net downhill.
Logistics nightmare: Rims are 220 miles apart by road (4.5 hrs). Use Trans-Canyon Shuttle ($100 one-way) to get your car.
Book Phantom Ranch 15+ months ahead. Lottery opens the 1st of each month for dates 15 months out.
Best season: Late September–October or May. Avoid summer heat at all costs.

trails.name: Havasu Falls (Havasupai)
trails.description: Turquoise waterfalls in a red rock canyon. Managed by the Havasupai Tribe, NOT the National Park Service. 10mi hike each way to the campground/lodge. Permits sell out in minutes. This is a bucket-list destination but requires serious planning.
tips: Not technically in Grand Canyon NP — it's on Havasupai Tribal land. Different permits, different rules.
Permits launch Feb 1 and sell out in minutes. Have multiple devices ready. ~$400/person for 3 nights camping.
10-mile hike in with 2,000ft descent. Last 2 miles are flat. Start before dawn in summer.
Helicopter option: $85 each way (when running). Book through the Havasupai Tribe.

trails.name: Hermit Trail
trails.description: Unmaintained but well-traveled "wilderness" trail. Much less crowded than the corridor trails. Starts at Hermit's Rest at the end of Hermit Road. The trail is rougher and steeper than Bright Angel but rewards with solitude. Connects to Tonto Trail.
tips: Day hike option: Hike to Santa Maria Spring (5mi RT, 1,680ft) for a satisfying turnaround with a rest shelter.
Not maintained but well-traveled. Expect loose rock and exposure. Route-finding skills helpful below Dripping Springs.
Hermit Creek campsite is gorgeous but has limited reliable water. Check with the Backcountry Office before going.
Trailhead at Hermit's Rest: Take the free Hermit Road shuttle (Red Route) March–November.

trails.name: Grandview Trail
trails.description: Steep, rocky, unmaintained trail to Horseshoe Mesa. One of the park's original tourist trails (1893). Incredible views and old copper mine ruins on the mesa. Challenging but doable as a day hike for experienced hikers. No water.
tips: Horseshoe Mesa is the goal. Copper mine ruins, 360° views, and a feeling of real remoteness.
Steep and loose cobblestones for the first mile. Trekking poles are essential, not optional.
No water anywhere. Carry 3+ liters. Start early.
Great alternative to the crowded corridor trails if you're an experienced hiker. Far fewer people.

trails.name: Tonto Trail
trails.description: The Grand Canyon's great east-west trail. Traverses the Tonto Platform ~3,000ft below the rim. Connects most of the inner canyon trails. Usually hiked in sections, not end-to-end. Exposed, hot, minimal water. Serious backcountry experience required.
tips: Most people hike sections, not the whole thing. Popular: Indian Garden to Hermit Creek (12mi) or to Horseshoe Mesa (18mi).
Fully exposed with zero shade. Summer temperatures on the Tonto Platform regularly exceed 110°F.
Water sources are unreliable and far apart. Research current conditions with the Backcountry Office before every trip.
Navigation can be tricky in side canyons. Carry a topo map and GPS. The trail fades in places.

trails.name: South Bass Trail
trails.description: Remote, rugged trail requiring high-clearance 4WD to reach the trailhead. Historic route built by William Bass in the 1890s. True wilderness experience with almost no other hikers. Bass Camp at the river has a beach and old cable crossing.
tips: 4WD required for the 30-mile dirt road to the trailhead. Check road conditions with the Backcountry Office.
Truly remote. If something goes wrong, help is very far away. Not for inexperienced canyon hikers.
No reliable water until the river. Carry everything you need for the descent.
Best for experienced backpackers who want Grand Canyon solitude that the corridor trails can't offer.

trails.name: Cape Royal Trail
trails.description: Paved trail to arguably the best viewpoint on the North Rim. Panoramic views of the canyon, the Colorado River, and Angels Window (a natural arch). Short enough for anyone. Drive Cape Royal Road to get here.
tips: Best North Rim viewpoint, period. You can see the river, Angels Window, and the entire eastern canyon.
Sunset here is incredible — but you'll have a 23-mile drive back to the lodge in the dark. Bring a flashlight.
Combine with Angels Window Overlook (separate short spur trail in the parking lot) for a view through the arch.
Accessible for wheelchairs and strollers. Fully paved.

trails.name: Transept Trail
trails.description: Connects Grand Canyon Lodge to North Rim Campground along the rim of Transept Canyon. Gentle walk through ponderosa pines with canyon views. Great morning or evening stroll. Look for Ancestral Puebloan ruins.
tips: Sunset walk from the lodge to the campground is lovely. Canyon glows orange and red.
Look for the small Puebloan ruin partway along the trail — most people walk right past it.
Great for deer and turkey sightings in the morning and at dusk. Kaibab squirrels (white-tailed) are endemic to the North Rim.

trails.name: Widforss Trail
trails.description: Best full-length day hike on the North Rim. Follows the rim of Transept Canyon through mixed forest to Widforss Point, with a stunning canyon view at the end. Named for artist Gunnar Widforss. Mostly flat with gentle undulations.
tips: The payoff is at the end. The first 3 miles are mostly in forest. Keep going — the final viewpoint is world-class.
Picnic at Widforss Point. Bring lunch and enjoy one of the most peaceful canyon views anywhere.
Fall colors (late Sep–Oct) make this trail spectacular. Aspens turn gold along the route.
Self-guided nature trail brochure available at the trailhead. Numbered posts match the guide.

trails.name: Uncle Jim Trail
trails.description: Loop trail to Uncle Jim Point overlooking the switchbacks of the North Kaibab Trail. Shares the first mile with North Kaibab. Great views of Roaring Springs Canyon. Watch mule trains navigate the switchbacks below you.
tips: Best view of the North Kaibab switchbacks. Watch hikers and mule trains zigzag 3,000ft below.
Start at the North Kaibab Trailhead parking lot. Trail shares the first section, then splits left for Uncle Jim.
Morning light is best for photos from Uncle Jim Point. The canyon is in shadow by afternoon.
Mule rides use this trail. Give mules right-of-way and follow ranger instructions.

trails.name: Clear Creek Trail
trails.description: Side trail from Phantom Ranch area to Clear Creek. Outstanding wilderness camping at the creek with seasonal waterfall. The trail traverses high above the Colorado River with views that few visitors ever see. CCC-built trail from the 1930s.
tips: Best as an add-on if you're already at Phantom Ranch. Take a rest day and make it a day hike or overnight.
Cheyava Falls (seasonal) is a 1-mile side trip from Clear Creek camp. Only flows during spring snowmelt.
Exposed traverse with significant drop-offs. Not for anyone uncomfortable with heights.
Water at Clear Creek only. Carry enough for the approach from Phantom Ranch.

trails.name: Thunder River Trail
trails.description: One of the most spectacular backcountry routes in the canyon. Thunder River is the shortest river in the world — it bursts from a cave in the cliff wall and plummets as a waterfall into Tapeats Creek. Remote and challenging, starting from the North Rim via the Bill Hall Trail.
tips: Thunder River bursts from a cave in the Redwall Limestone. It's a half-mile long and drops 1,200ft. Nothing else like it.
Usually combined with Bill Hall Trail for the descent from the rim. Total of ~24mi round trip.
Best in May or September–October. Summer heat in the inner canyon is brutal.
Drive to Indian Hollow Trailhead requires high-clearance vehicle on Forest Road 232. Check road conditions.

trails.name: Bill Hall Trail
trails.description: North Rim connector trail to Thunder River and Deer Creek. Descends through the Esplanade Sandstone and Supai Group with massive views. Starting point for the Thunder River / Deer Creek loop. The trail drops through a break in the Redwall cliff.
tips: Gateway to Thunder River. Most people combine Bill Hall + Thunder River as a 3–4 day trip.
The Redwall descent is steep and exposed. Not for those with vertigo.
Indian Hollow Trailhead is the starting point. ~30mi of dirt road from the paved highway. Check conditions.
Carry all your water from the trailhead until you reach Thunder River or Tapeats Creek.

trails.name: Plateau Point
trails.description: Spur off Bright Angel Trail at Havasupai Gardens, extending to the edge of the inner gorge with a direct view 1,300ft down to the Colorado River. Extremely demanding day hike. The return climb from Havasupai Gardens to the rim is the hardest part — 4.6 miles and 3,060ft straight up.
tips: Do NOT attempt in summer. NPS explicitly warns against it. Hikers regularly need helicopter rescue.
Start before dawn. You need to be back at Havasupai Gardens before the afternoon heat hits.
Carry 4+ liters of water and salty snacks. Hyponatremia (too much water, not enough salt) is a real risk.
The view of the river from the point is one of the best in the park. But earn it on a cool day.

trails.name: Cedar Ridge (South Kaibab)
trails.description: Hike the South Kaibab Trail to its best day-hike turnaround. Cedar Ridge is a flat, open area with panoramic views, composting toilets, and a hitching post for mules. You get the South Kaibab experience without committing to the full descent.
tips: Best short below-the-rim hike. All the views, fraction of the commitment. Smart choice for most visitors.
Take the Orange (Kaibab/Rim) shuttle to the trailhead. No private vehicle parking.
Even this "short" hike is harder than it looks. The 1,120ft climb back out in thin air is no joke. Bring water.
Composting toilets at Cedar Ridge. Only restroom below the rim until you reach Havasupai Gardens on Bright Angel.

trails.name: Skeleton Point (South Kaibab)
trails.description: Continue past Cedar Ridge for the first views of the Colorado River on the South Kaibab Trail. Skeleton Point is a promontory with a direct view 2,000ft down to the river. This is the recommended turnaround for strong hikers who want more without going all the way.
tips: First river views on South Kaibab. You can see the Bright Angel Creek confluence and Phantom Ranch area.
No water, no shade past Cedar Ridge. The sun is relentless. Start early.
Avoid May–September. The 2,000ft climb back to the rim in summer heat is where rescues happen.
Great goal for fit hikers who want a challenging day hike without needing a backcountry permit.

uniqueSections.title: Rim to Rim Planning
uniqueSections.content: The classic Grand Canyon through-hike: 21–24 miles from one rim to the other, crossing the Colorado River and the billion-year geological record.

bottomUp.title: South to North (Most Common)
bottomUp.description: Start at South Kaibab Trailhead, cross the Colorado via Black Bridge, ascend North Kaibab Trail. Steeper descent, more gradual climb out.

milestones.name: Phantom to Cottonwood
milestones.note: Gradual ascent along Bright Angel Creek

topDown.title: North to South
topDown.route: North Kaibab to Phantom Ranch to Bright Angel Trail
highlights: Longer descent gives more time to enjoy the scenery
Ascend Bright Angel Trail — has water stops at 3-Mile and 1.5-Mile Resthouses
End at South Rim with shuttle access and services
Trans-Canyon Shuttle ($100 one-way) runs between rims May–October

bestTimes.note: Best weather. 50–70°F at rim, 70–80°F at river. Fall colors on North Rim.
Wildflowers, comfortable temps, fewer crowds than fall.
Inner canyon reaches 110°F+. Heat exhaustion is almost guaranteed for multi-day trips.

uniqueSections.title: South Rim vs North Rim
uniqueSections.content: Two completely different experiences of the same canyon.

bottomUp.title: South Rim
bottomUp.description: The accessible, year-round rim. 90% of visitors come here. Grand Canyon Village has lodges, restaurants, shuttle buses, and the most iconic viewpoints.
highlights: Open year-round. All services available March–October, reduced in winter.
Elevation: 7,000ft. Hot summers (90°F+), cold winters (snow common).
Viewpoints: Mather Point, Yavapai Point, Hopi Point, Desert View Watchtower.
Trails: Bright Angel and South Kaibab — the two main corridor trails into the canyon.
Getting there: 80 mi from Flagstaff (1.5 hrs), 230 mi from Phoenix (3.5 hrs), 270 mi from Las Vegas (4.5 hrs).
Best for: First-timers, families, day hikers, anyone who wants full services.

topDown.title: North Rim
topDown.distance: 215 miles by road from South Rim (4+ hours)
highlights: Open mid-May to mid-October only. Road closes at first significant snowfall.
Elevation: 8,200ft. Cooler temps (10–15°F less than South Rim). Ponderosa pine forests.
Viewpoints: Bright Angel Point, Cape Royal, Point Imperial (highest point on either rim at 8,803ft).
Crowds: Only 10% of park visitors come here. Genuinely peaceful.
Getting there: 350 mi from Las Vegas (5 hrs), 350 mi from Phoenix (5.5 hrs). No airports nearby.
Best for: Solitude seekers, photographers, fall color chasers, experienced hikers wanting the North Kaibab Trail.

uniqueSections.title: Phantom Ranch
uniqueSections.content: The only lodging below the rim — and one of the hardest reservations in the National Park System.

bottomUp.title: The Experience
bottomUp.description: Built in 1922 by architect Mary Colter, Phantom Ranch sits at the bottom of the Grand Canyon along Bright Angel Creek, near the Colorado River. It's accessible only by foot, mule, or raft.
highlights: Cabins: 11 cabins (4 dorm-style, 7 private). Historic stone buildings with beds, linens, and towels.
Meals: Steak dinner ($56), hiker stew ($31), breakfast ($28). Must reserve meals with lodging.
Canteen: Lemonade, snacks, and the famous Phantom Ranch postcard — mailed out by mule.
Atmosphere: No cell service, no Wi-Fi, no TV. Stars, creek sounds, and canyon walls.

topDown.title: How to Book
highlights: Lottery opens on the 1st of each month for stays 15 months out. Apply at grandcanyonlodges.com.
Odds are low. Tens of thousands of entries for ~90 available beds per night.
Cancellation waitlist: Call (888) 297-2757 starting 48 hours before desired date. Cancelations do happen, especially midweek.
Alternative: Camp at Bright Angel Campground (33 sites, next to Phantom Ranch) with a backcountry permit. Easier to get.
Mule trips: Ride a mule down from South Rim. Includes 1 night at Phantom Ranch. Book at grandcanyonlodges.com — also a lottery.

camping.name: Mather Campground
camping.description: The big South Rim campground. Walking distance to Market Plaza, shuttle stops, and the rim. Sites are in pinyon-juniper forest. Showers and laundry at the nearby Camper Services building.

camping.name: Desert View Campground
camping.description: First-come, first-served. Near Desert View Watchtower, 25 miles east of Grand Canyon Village. Quieter and less crowded than Mather. No hookups, no showers. Fills by noon in peak season.

camping.name: North Rim Campground
camping.description: The only campground on the North Rim. Walk to the lodge and rim viewpoints. In ponderosa pine forest at 8,200ft. Much cooler than the South Rim. Book months ahead for summer.

camping.name: Bright Angel Campground
camping.description: At the bottom of the canyon, next to Phantom Ranch. Cottonwood trees along Bright Angel Creek. Year-round water and toilets. Requires backcountry permit. The reward for hiking 7+ miles and 4,000ft down.

camping.name: Havasupai Gardens (Indian Garden)
camping.description: Halfway point on Bright Angel Trail. Cottonwood oasis with year-round water, toilets, and ranger station. Great base for a day hike to Plateau Point. Backcountry permit required.

camping.name: Dispersed Camping — Kaibab National Forest
camping.description: Free dispersed camping in Kaibab National Forest, both north and south of the park. South side: along Forest Road 302 (Grandview Lookout area) or FR 307. North side: along AZ-67 or FR 22. No facilities. 14-day limit.

insiderTips.title: October is the sweet spot.
insiderTips.detail: Summer crowds are gone, South Rim is 60–70°F, fall colors on the North Rim, and Phantom Ranch is still bookable.

insiderTips.title: Sunrise at Mather Point, sunset at Hopi Point.
insiderTips.detail: These are the classic combos and they're classic for a reason.

insiderTips.title: The Hermit Road shuttle is free sightseeing.
insiderTips.detail: 7 viewpoints in 7 miles. Ride the whole loop or hop off at any stop.

insiderTips.title: South Kaibab down, Bright Angel up.
insiderTips.detail: This is the way. Views on the descent, water on the ascent.

insiderTips.title: Desert View Watchtower is worth the 25-mile drive.
insiderTips.detail: Mary Colter's 1932 masterpiece. Hopi murals inside. Best gift shop in the park.

insiderTips.title: Skip the IMAX theater.
insiderTips.detail: You're at the actual Grand Canyon. Walk to the rim instead.

insiderTips.title: Phantom Ranch lemonade is legendary.
insiderTips.detail: Even if you're just passing through, stop for a glass. $3 well spent.

insiderTips.title: The "rule of thirds":
insiderTips.detail: For every 1 hour hiking down, budget 2 hours hiking up. Your legs will thank you.

insiderTips.title: Mule trips book up 13+ months ahead
insiderTips.detail: for the overnight to Phantom Ranch. Day rides along the rim are easier to get.

insiderTips.title: Free ranger programs
insiderTips.detail: happen daily at both rims. Evening geology talks are outstanding. Check the park newspaper.

insiderTips.title: The North Rim gets 10% of the visitors.
insiderTips.detail: If you want Grand Canyon without the crowds, drive the extra 200 miles.

insiderTips.title: Yavapai Geology Museum
insiderTips.detail: has the best educational displays in the park. Start here to understand what you're looking at.

insiderTips.title: Helicopter tours from Tusayan
insiderTips.detail: are ~$200/person for 25–45 min. Papillon and Maverick are the big operators. Worth it once.

insiderTips.title: Water bottles fill up everywhere
insiderTips.detail: on the South Rim. Bring a reusable bottle — no disposable water bottles sold in the park.

always: 1 gallon (4L) of water per person per day below the rim
Electrolyte tablets or salty snacks (hyponatremia is real)
Sturdy hiking boots with ankle support
Sun protection: wide-brim hat, SPF 50+, sunglasses, sun-protective clothing
Headlamp with extra batteries (for pre-dawn starts)
First aid kit with blister supplies and moleskin
Offline maps downloaded to phone + paper map
belowRim: Water purification (filter or tablets) for creek water
Extra food beyond what you think you need
Compression socks for the climb out
seasonal: Summer: Cooling towel, spray bottle, electrolyte powder, lightweight long sleeves
Winter: Microspikes for icy rim trails, warm layers, insulated water bottle
Monsoon (Jul–Sep): Rain jacket, lightning awareness, check flash flood potential

nearPark.name: We Cook Pizza & Pasta (Tusayan)
nearPark.description: Decent pizza after a long day. Nothing fancy but hits the spot. Walking distance from most Tusayan hotels.

nearPark.name: Plaza Bonita (Tusayan)
nearPark.description: Mexican food with generous portions. Margaritas are strong. Open late by canyon standards.

nearPark.name: Rod's Steak House (Williams)
nearPark.description: Route 66 classic since 1946. Steaks and no-frills Americana. Worth the 60-mile drive from the South Rim.

nearPark.name: Cruiser's Cafe 66 (Williams)
nearPark.description: Route 66 themed burger joint. Good shakes. Fun for families.

groceries.name: Market Plaza General Store
groceries.description: South Rim. Basic groceries, camping supplies, and deli items. Expensive — stock up in Flagstaff or Williams before arriving.

groceries.name: North Rim General Store
groceries.description: Very limited selection. Only option on the North Rim.

restaurants.name: El Tovar Dining Room
restaurants.description: The grand dame. Best restaurant in the park. Reservations essential. Try the elk stew or the salmon. Breakfast is more accessible — show up at 6:30 AM.

restaurants.name: Arizona Room
restaurants.description: Steaks and ribs with canyon views. No reservations — put your name on the list and walk the rim while you wait. Opens at 4:30 PM.

restaurants.name: Bright Angel Lodge Restaurant
restaurants.description: Casual, reliable, family-friendly. Good breakfast. Harvey Burger is the move. Open early for hikers.

restaurants.name: Yavapai Lodge Restaurant
restaurants.description: Tavern-style pub food. Pizza, burgers, salads. Near Market Plaza. Least impressive food but most convenient.

restaurants.name: Phantom Ranch Canteen
restaurants.description: At the bottom of the canyon. Pre-order steak ($55) or stew ($25) dinner, served family-style. Breakfast is eggs and pancakes. Sack lunches available. Reserve when you book Phantom Ranch.

restaurants.name: Grand Canyon Lodge Dining Room (North Rim)
restaurants.description: Only sit-down restaurant on the North Rim. Floor-to-ceiling canyon views. Reserve early. Breakfast buffet is solid. Open mid-May to mid-October.

inPark.name: El Tovar Hotel
inPark.description: Historic 1905 lodge right on the rim. The best address at Grand Canyon. Book the day reservations open.

inPark.name: Bright Angel Lodge
inPark.description: Historic lodge with budget-friendly rim cabins and "Buckey" O'Neill cabin (splurge). Some rooms share bathrooms.

inPark.name: Kachina Lodge
inPark.description: Modern rooms, some with canyon views. Best value for rim-side lodging.

inPark.name: Thunderbird Lodge
inPark.description: Sister property to Kachina. Similar rooms and location. Canyon-side rooms are worth the upgrade.

inPark.name: Maswik Lodge
inPark.description: Set back from the rim in the pines. More affordable. North and South buildings — South is newer and nicer.

inPark.name: Phantom Ranch
inPark.description: Only lodging below the rim. Dorms ($64) or cabins ($180). Lottery opens on the 1st of each month for dates 15 months out.

inPark.name: Grand Canyon Lodge (North Rim)
inPark.description: Historic lodge with cabins. Western Cabins have canyon views. Open mid-May to mid-October. Book early.

nearPark.name: Tusayan (1mi from South Entrance)
nearPark.description: Best Western, Holiday Inn Express, Grand Hotel. Expensive but convenient. Walk to the IMAX theater and shuttle.

nearPark.name: Williams (60mi south)
nearPark.description: Route 66 town. Budget motels + Grand Canyon Railway. Take the train to the rim for a fun day trip.

nearPark.name: Flagstaff (80mi south)
nearPark.description: Real city with restaurants, Walmart, REI. Best place to stock up. Hotels are more affordable than Tusayan.

items.title: Sunrise at Mather Point
items.description: Arrive before dawn. Watch the canyon light up. Mather Point is a 5-minute walk from the main parking lot.

items.title: Yavapai Geology Museum
items.description: Understand the layers. Best educational stop in the park. Free.

items.title: Walk Rim Trail to Bright Angel Lodge
items.description: 1.5-mile paved walk along the rim. Stop at Verkamp's Visitor Center.

items.title: Hermit Road Shuttle
items.description: Ride the free Red Route shuttle. Get off at Powell Point and Hopi Point for the best views.

items.title: Lunch at Bright Angel Lodge
items.description: Harvey Burger and done. Or picnic on the rim.

items.title: Sunrise at Yaki Point
items.description: Take Orange shuttle to Yaki Point for sunrise. Then continue to South Kaibab Trailhead.

items.title: Descend South Kaibab to Cedar Ridge
items.description: 1.5mi down, 1,120ft. Panoramic ridge views the entire way. 45–60 min.

items.title: Continue to Skeleton Point (optional)
items.description: 3mi from rim, 2,040ft down. First views of the river. Only if you're a strong hiker. Adds 2–3hr.

items.title: Back at the Rim
items.description: Climb back up South Kaibab. Brutal but short. Celebrate at the top.

items.title: Market Plaza
items.description: Resupply, shower at Camper Services if needed, lunch at Yavapai Lodge.

items.title: Desert View Drive
items.description: Drive east to Desert View Watchtower (25mi). Stop at Lipan Point and Grandview Point.

items.title: Return to Village
items.description: Back along Desert View Drive.

items.title: Sunset at Hopi Point
items.description: Take Hermit Road shuttle. Arrive 45 min early for a spot.

items.title: Descend South Kaibab
items.description: 7mi to Phantom Ranch. 4,780ft descent. Take it slow. 4–6 hours.

items.title: Arrive at Phantom Ranch
items.description: Check in. Explore. Wade in Bright Angel Creek. Buy lemonade.

items.title: Dinner at the Canteen
items.description: Family-style steak or stew. Beer and wine available.

items.title: Stars
items.description: Zero light pollution. The Milky Way is outrageous from the inner canyon.

items.title: Breakfast at the Canteen
items.description: Eggs, pancakes, coffee. Fuel up.

items.title: Ascend Bright Angel Trail
items.description: 9.5mi to South Rim. 4,380ft gain. Fill water at Havasupai Gardens, 3-Mile, and 1.5-Mile (seasonal).

items.title: Arrive at Rim
items.description: Celebrate. Eat everything. Nap.

items.title: Drive to North Rim
items.description: Stay at North Rim Lodge or campground. Pick up Trans-Canyon Shuttle ticket for return.

items.title: Descend North Kaibab
items.description: 14mi to Phantom Ranch/Bright Angel CG. 5,761ft descent. Long day. Fill water at Supai Tunnel, Roaring Springs, Manzanita, Cottonwood.

items.title: Arrive at Phantom Ranch
items.description: Rest, swim, eat. Celebrate the halfway point.

items.title: Rest Day or Clear Creek
items.description: Optional day hike to Clear Creek (18mi RT) or just rest at the ranch. You earned it.

items.title: Ascend Bright Angel Trail
items.description: 9.5mi, 4,380ft gain to South Rim. Start early, take breaks, drink water.

items.title: South Rim
items.description: Arrive at South Rim. Trans-Canyon Shuttle back to North Rim (~4.5hr drive, $100).

details.description: Variable weather. Snow possible on rim into April. Best wildflower season in inner canyon. North Rim opens mid-May. Crowds: Building.
Inner canyon is DANGEROUS. Monsoon thunderstorms July–September. Lightning on rim. Flash flood risk. Both rims open. Crowds: Peak.
Best overall season. October is ideal. North Rim fall colors. Crowds thin dramatically after Labor Day. North Rim closes mid-October. Crowds: Decreasing.
South Rim only. Snow on rim creates stunning photos. Inner canyon temps are pleasant for hiking. Icy rim trails require microspikes. North Rim road closed. Crowds: Low.

photography.notes: Most iconic sunrise spot. Arrive 30–45 min before dawn. Gets crowded but the view is unbeatable.
Widest canyon view from the South Rim. Take the Hermit Road shuttle. Arrive early for the best ledge spots.
Best view of the Colorado River from Desert View Drive. Less crowded than Mather. Wide panorama.
Mary Colter's 1932 stone tower. Photograph the tower AND the canyon. Interior murals are worth a photo.
Geology Museum is here. Good sunrise alternative to Mather with slightly fewer people.
3,000ft sheer cliff to the river. Most dramatic viewpoint but requires 60+ miles of dirt road and a backcountry permit. No guardrails.
Angels Window natural arch. Best North Rim photography. Long drive back in the dark.

hiddenGems.name: Shoshone Point
hiddenGems.description: Unmarked 1-mile walk through ponderosa pine to a secluded rim viewpoint. No crowds, no signs. Popular for weddings. The trailhead is an unmarked pulloff on Desert View Drive.

hiddenGems.name: Hermit's Rest
hiddenGems.description: Mary Colter's quirky 1914 rest stop at the end of Hermit Road. Designed to look like a hermit's stone shelter. Great hot cocoa and snacks. Most people just ride the shuttle past.

hiddenGems.name: Toroweap / Tuweep
hiddenGems.description: The most dramatic viewpoint: 3,000ft sheer drop to the river. Requires 60+ miles of dirt road, high clearance vehicle, and self-sufficiency. 9 primitive campsites (free, first-come). Pure solitude.

hiddenGems.name: North Rim (in general)
hiddenGems.description: 10% of Grand Canyon visitors see the North Rim. It's higher (8,241ft), cooler, more forested, and profoundly quieter. The lodge patio at sunset is one of the great park experiences.

hiddenGems.name: Ribbon Falls (North Kaibab Trail)
hiddenGems.description: Travertine waterfall in a grotto along the North Kaibab Trail. 0.5-mile spur off the main trail. Most hikers are too focused on reaching Phantom Ranch to stop. Their loss.

scenicDrives.name: Desert View Drive (East Rim)
scenicDrives.description: South Rim road east to Desert View Watchtower. Seven viewpoints including Grandview, Lipan, and Desert View. Less crowded than Hermit Road. Drive your own car year-round.

scenicDrives.name: Hermit Road (West Rim)
scenicDrives.description: South Rim road west to Hermit's Rest. Eight viewpoints including Hopi, Mohave, and Pima. Shuttle-only March–November (free). Open to private vehicles in winter.

scenicDrives.name: Cape Royal Road (North Rim)
scenicDrives.description: North Rim drive to Cape Royal and Point Imperial. Highest point on either rim (8,803ft). Through ponderosa and aspen forest. Open mid-May to mid-October.

safety.details: Inner canyon temps exceed 120°F in summer. Carry 1 gallon of water per person per day below the rim. Hike during cooler hours (before 10 AM, after 4 PM). Symptoms: nausea, dizziness, cessation of sweating. Get into shade immediately and cool down.

safety.hazard: Do NOT Hike Rim-to-River-to-Rim in One Day
safety.details: Every year, fit hikers attempt this and end up needing helicopter rescue or die. The NPS explicitly warns against it. The 4,000–5,000ft climb out in extreme heat is the killer. Plan an overnight or turn around at a resthouse.
Drinking too much water without electrolytes dilutes blood sodium. Symptoms mimic heat exhaustion. Eat salty snacks with every water intake. This kills people at Grand Canyon.
About 12 people die at Grand Canyon each year; 2–3 from falls over the rim. Stay on maintained trails. Do not sit on ledges with your feet dangling. Watch children at ALL times.
Monsoon season (July–September) brings sudden, violent thunderstorms. Flash floods can roar through side canyons with no warning. Never camp in dry washes. Check weather before inner canyon trips.
The rim is extremely exposed. During monsoon storms, get away from the rim edge, isolated trees, and metal structures. Crouch low if trapped in the open.
Rock squirrels bite. Elk can be aggressive in rut (Sept–Oct). Rattlesnakes and scorpions on trails, especially below the rim. Mountain lions are present but rarely seen.

emergency.nearestHospital: Flagstaff Medical Center — 1200 N Beaver St, Flagstaff, AZ 86001 (~80 min from South Rim)
emergency.notes: The South Rim has a small clinic (walk-in, limited hours). North Rim has no medical facility. For life-threatening emergencies below the rim, NPS Search & Rescue may deploy helicopters. Carry a personal locator beacon (PLB) for backcountry trips.

commonMistakes.title: Hiking Rim to River and Back in One Day
commonMistakes.detail: The #1 mistake. It's twice as far and three times as hard as it looks. NPS says: DO NOT attempt this, especially May–September. Plan an overnight or choose a turnaround point (Cedar Ridge, 3-Mile Resthouse).

commonMistakes.title: Not Carrying Enough Water
commonMistakes.detail: Below the rim, you need 1 gallon per person per day. A 500ml bottle from the gift shop won't cut it. Bring a hydration pack and fill at every water source.

commonMistakes.title: Starting a Below-Rim Hike at Noon
commonMistakes.detail: Inner canyon temps peak at 2–4 PM. Start before dawn. If you're just starting at lunch, you're doing it wrong.

commonMistakes.title: Only Visiting Mather Point
commonMistakes.detail: It's the first viewpoint and most people never leave. Walk the Rim Trail. Take the Hermit Road shuttle. Drive Desert View Drive. The canyon is 277 miles long.

commonMistakes.title: Skipping the North Rim
commonMistakes.detail: Yes, it's a 4.5-hour drive from the South Rim. But it's a completely different experience: quieter, cooler, more forested. 90% of visitors never see it.

commonMistakes.title: Wearing Flip-Flops Below the Rim
commonMistakes.detail: People do this. Don't be people. Proper hiking boots with ankle support are non-negotiable on any below-rim trail.

commonMistakes.title: Not Making Reservations
commonMistakes.detail: El Tovar, Phantom Ranch, and both campgrounds book out months to over a year in advance. Winging it at Grand Canyon is a recipe for sleeping in your car in Tusayan.

commonMistakes.title: Ignoring the Elevation
commonMistakes.detail: South Rim is at 6,860ft. North Rim is at 8,241ft. If you're coming from sea level, you'll feel it. Stay hydrated and take it easy the first day.
